title: Why some cities deserve to be illegible
summary: Argue that cities which resist easy navigation and orientation offer something that optimised, legible cities cannot.
topic: Why some cities deserve to be illegible
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) defending the idea that cities which are disorienting, labyrinthine, or deliberately hard to read — such as old medinas, historic Japanese towns, or warren-like European quarters — offer something valuable that optimised, grid-based, GPS-friendly cities erase. Use a cleft sentence, a concession ('Admittedly, …' or 'Granted, …'), at least one passive construction, and a participle clause. Include at least one concrete urban example. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What is lost when a city is made fully legible is not merely a sense of mystery but…
Admittedly, a city that cannot be navigated without a guide imposes real costs on…
Stripped of its confusing alleys and unmarked dead ends, the old quarter would become…

hintVocab.meaningVi: khó đọc / khó định hướng (nói về đô thị)
khả năng đọc / dễ định hướng trong không gian đô thị
như mê cung / rất phức tạp và khó đi
tìm đường / định hướng trong không gian
gánh nặng nhận thức
mất phương hướng trong không gian
tối ưu hóa (một thành phố)
cấu trúc hạt nhỏ của đô thị (mạng lưới phố, lô đất)
quy hoạch dạng lưới ô vuông
được làm cho dễ đi lại
sự tình cờ may mắn / khám phá bất ngờ thú vị
lang thang / đi theo đường quanh co
bị tước bỏ / bị lấy đi
ma sát (đô thị) — sự kháng cự nhẹ trong trải nghiệm đô thị
người dạo phố — kẻ lang thang tự do trong đô thị (từ tiếng Pháp)
biến thành hàng hóa / thương mại hóa
khu di sản
bị đồng nhất hóa
tính cách đô thị / bản sắc của một thành phố
chống lại sự dễ đọc / dễ định hướng
ngưỡng cửa / điểm chuyển tiếp không gian
xóa bỏ / làm mờ đi

sampleText:
What is lost when a city is made fully legible is not merely a sense of mystery but the very conditions under which genuine discovery becomes possible. The medina of Fez, with its 9,000 lanes and its systematic refusal to accommodate a straight line, cannot be understood from above. It must be entered, got lost in, and re-entered from a different gate. The knowledge it yields is earned, not downloaded.

Urban planners have long treated legibility as an unqualified good: a city that can be read, mapped, and traversed efficiently is, in this view, a city that works. Kevin Lynch's influential 1960 framework measured cities by how clearly their structure was perceived by residents. What it did not measure was the cost of that clarity — what was erased in the process of being made easy.

Admittedly, a city that cannot be navigated without a guide imposes real costs on its residents. The narrow lanes of Fez are poorly served by ambulances. Elderly residents in the old quarter of Naples describe the labyrinthine streets as exhausting rather than charming. For people with visual impairments or mobility constraints, illegibility is not an aesthetic category — it is an exclusion. These are not abstract concerns, and any defence of urban complexity that ignores them is irresponsible.

Even so, the answer to these problems is targeted accessibility intervention, not wholesale spatial rationalisation. Stripped of its confusing alleys and unmarked dead ends, the old quarter would become a theme park version of itself: legible, navigable, and entirely without surprise. The friction that makes such places difficult is inseparable from the friction that makes them alive. A city in which every corner is anticipated is a city that has nothing left to offer after the first visit.

The deeper argument for illegibility is that it resists the kind of frictionless consumption that makes cities interchangeable. Tokyo's historic Yanaka district, having survived both earthquake and wartime bombing, retains a grain so fine and irregular that no map conveys it accurately. To walk through it is to be reminded that a city is not an interface — it is an environment that was built by people who did not yet know what you would need from it. That gap between past intention and present use is precisely where urban life happens. Legibility, taken too far, closes that gap permanently.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: sự từ chối có hệ thống việc chứa đựng một đường thẳng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The knowledge it yields is earned, not downloaded
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Kiến thức mà nó mang lại được kiếm bằng nỗ lực, không phải tải xuống

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Urban planners have long treated legibility as an unqualified good

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: những gì đã bị xóa trong quá trình được làm cho dễ dàng

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to expose the hidden loss in urban optimisation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What is lost when a city is made fully legible is not merely a sense of mystery but the very conditions under which genuine discovery becomes possible' dùng 'What … is …' để trì hoãn kết luận quan trọng nhất — không chỉ là bí ẩn mà là 'điều kiện để khám phá' — đến cuối câu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What is lost when a city is made fully legible is not merely … but the very conditions …' uses a what-cleft to reveal what is truly at stake — not atmosphere, but the structural preconditions for discovery — only at the sentence's end, maximising its impact.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Participle clause compressing prior history into a modifier
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'having survived both earthquake and wartime bombing, retains a grain so fine and irregular …' dùng mệnh đề phân từ hoàn thành ('having + V3') để nén lịch sử dài vào một cụm từ, làm cho sự tồn tại của khu phố trở nên đặc biệt hơn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'having survived both earthquake and wartime bombing, retains a grain …' compresses decades of history into a participle clause, making the survival of the district's irregular grain feel earned and historically charged without needing a separate sentence.

sampleTranslationVi:
Điều bị mất khi một thành phố được làm cho hoàn toàn dễ đọc không chỉ là cảm giác bí ẩn mà còn là những điều kiện để khám phá thực sự trở nên khả thi. Khu medina của Fez, với 9.000 con đường và sự từ chối có hệ thống việc chứa đựng một đường thẳng, không thể được hiểu từ trên cao. Nó phải được đi vào, bị lạc trong đó, và vào lại từ một cổng khác. Kiến thức mà nó mang lại được kiếm bằng nỗ lực, không phải tải xuống.

Các nhà quy hoạch đô thị từ lâu đã coi tính dễ đọc là một điều tốt hoàn toàn không cần bàn cãi: một thành phố có thể được đọc, lập bản đồ và đi qua một cách hiệu quả là, theo quan điểm này, một thành phố hoạt động tốt. Khung nghiên cứu có ảnh hưởng năm 1960 của Kevin Lynch đo lường các thành phố bằng mức độ cư dân nhận thức rõ ràng cấu trúc của chúng. Điều nó không đo lường được là cái giá của sự rõ ràng đó — những gì đã bị xóa trong quá trình được làm cho dễ dàng.

Thừa nhận, một thành phố không thể đi lại mà không có người dẫn đường áp đặt chi phí thực sự lên cư dân của nó. Những con đường hẹp của Fez phục vụ kém cho xe cứu thương. Cư dân cao tuổi ở khu phố cổ Naples mô tả những con phố như mê cung là mệt mỏi chứ không phải quyến rũ. Đối với những người khiếm thị hoặc bị hạn chế về khả năng di chuyển, tính khó đọc không phải là một phạm trù thẩm mỹ — đó là sự loại trừ. Đây không phải là những mối quan ngại trừu tượng, và bất kỳ sự bảo vệ nào về sự phức tạp đô thị mà bỏ qua chúng là vô trách nhiệm.

Dẫu vậy, câu trả lời cho những vấn đề này là can thiệp tiếp cận có mục tiêu, không phải hợp lý hóa không gian toàn diện. Bị tước bỏ những con hẻm khó hiểu và những con đường cụt không có biển hiệu, khu phố cổ sẽ trở thành phiên bản công viên giải trí của chính nó: dễ đọc, dễ đi lại, và hoàn toàn không có bất ngờ. Sự ma sát khiến những nơi đó khó khăn không thể tách rời khỏi sự ma sát khiến chúng sống động. Một thành phố mà mọi góc đường đều có thể đoán trước là một thành phố không còn gì để cung cấp sau lần ghé thăm đầu tiên.

Lập luận sâu sắc hơn cho tính khó đọc là nó chống lại kiểu tiêu dùng không có ma sát khiến các thành phố trở nên có thể hoán đổi cho nhau. Khu phố lịch sử Yanaka của Tokyo, đã sống sót qua cả động đất và ném bom trong thời chiến, giữ lại một cấu trúc hạt nhỏ đến mức không bản đồ nào truyền đạt chính xác. Đi bộ qua đó là được nhắc nhở rằng một thành phố không phải là một giao diện — đó là một môi trường được xây dựng bởi những người chưa biết bạn sẽ cần gì từ nó. Khoảng cách giữa ý định quá khứ và việc sử dụng hiện tại chính xác là nơi cuộc sống đô thị diễn ra. Tính dễ đọc, được đẩy đi quá xa, đóng khoảng cách đó vĩnh viễn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to reveal what is truly lost in urban optimisation?
options: What is lost when a city is made fully legible is not merely a sense of mystery but the very conditions under which genuine discovery becomes possible.
Legibility is lost when a city is made fully mysterious not discovery conditions possible.
It is mystery that is lost when conditions city legible discovery possible.
Cities lose what mystery legible made conditions discovery possible.
mcQuestions.explanation: The what-cleft delays the crucial term — 'the very conditions under which genuine discovery becomes possible' — to the end, where it carries maximum weight.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the key revelation at the very end.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a perfect participle clause to compress prior history?
options: Tokyo's Yanaka district, having survived both earthquake and wartime bombing, retains a grain so fine and irregular that no map conveys it accurately.
Survived having earthquake and wartime bombing Tokyo Yanaka retains grain no map accurately.
Having retained a grain Tokyo's Yanaka earthquake bombing survived fine irregular map.
Tokyo's Yanaka grain retained fine irregular having earthquake bombing survived map.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Having survived …' compresses the district's history into a participial phrase; its implied subject ('Yanaka') matches the main clause subject.
mcQuestions.hint: The participle clause subject must match the main clause subject — find the option where they align.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'illegibility' mean when applied to a city?
options: The quality of having poor street lighting
The difficulty of reading the signs in a city because they are in a foreign language
The property of a city that makes it difficult to navigate, orient oneself, or predict its layout
The formal classification of a city as a heritage site
mcQuestions.explanation: In urban studies (following Kevin Lynch), 'legibility' means the ease with which a city's structure can be mentally mapped; illegibility is the inverse.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'legible' meaning 'readable' — how does that apply to a city's spatial structure?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly foregrounds what has been erased rather than who did the erasing?
options: Urban planners erased everything in the process of making the city easy.
What was erased in the process of being made easy is not measured.
The erasing process made the city easy by urban planners working on it.
Being easy the city was made and urban planners erased things.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What was erased in the process of being made easy' uses two passives to keep focus on the thing lost, not on who did the erasing.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option avoids naming agents and keeps the focus on what was lost?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what is the correct response to the accessibility problems of illegible cities?
options: Demolishing old quarters and rebuilding them on a grid plan
Installing GPS kiosks throughout historic districts
Targeted accessibility intervention rather than wholesale spatial rationalisation
Restricting access to elderly and disabled residents until changes are made
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states: 'the answer to these problems is targeted accessibility intervention, not wholesale spatial rationalisation.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the sentence that follows 'Even so, the answer to these problems is…'

options: Careful advance planning that leads to a successful outcome
The pleasant discovery of something unexpected when not specifically looking for it
The formal process of mapping a new urban district
A strong emotional attachment to a familiar neighbourhood
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Serendipity' means a fortunate, unplanned discovery; in this context it describes what is only possible when a city has not been fully pre-mapped.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of stumbling upon something delightful without having searched for it — what is that called?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to commodify' mean in the context of urban heritage?
options: To list a building's architectural features in a formal inventory
To turn something with cultural or experiential value into a product to be sold
To restrict commercial activity in a historic district
To restore a heritage building using traditional materials
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Commodify' means to turn something into a commodity — a product whose value is measured by what it can be sold for, rather than by its intrinsic qualities.
mcQuestions.hint: A 'commodity' is a tradeable good — what happens when culture is treated as one?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'stripped of' correctly in a metaphorical sense?
options: Stripped of its confusing alleys and unmarked dead ends, the old quarter would become a theme park version of itself.
The old quarter stripped alleys confusing dead ends theme park version itself.
Confusing alleys stripped the old quarter of dead ends theme park becoming.
The theme park stripped confusing alleys old quarter dead ends version.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stripped of + noun phrase' is a participial construction meaning 'if/once its X is removed'; it introduces a hypothetical that then becomes the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: The phrase 'stripped of' introduces a condition — what would result if those things were removed?

options: The agricultural land surrounding a city
The texture of building materials used in historic construction
The fine-scale pattern of streets, lots, and blocks that gives a neighbourhood its physical character
The amount of green space within a city's boundaries
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Urban grain' describes the size and pattern of city blocks, plots, and street widths — coarse grain means large blocks; fine grain means many small, varied parcels.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of the texture of wood grain — 'fine' and 'irregular' are used as adjectives in the essay.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals an acknowledgement of the opposing argument before pivoting?
options: In contrast, a city that cannot be navigated without a guide imposes real costs.
Admittedly, a city that cannot be navigated without a guide imposes real costs on its residents.
Therefore, a city that cannot be navigated without a guide imposes real costs.
As a result, a city that cannot be navigated without a guide imposes real costs.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly, …' concedes a real objection while signalling that the writer has not abandoned their main claim.
mcQuestions.hint: Which single-word opener most naturally signals 'I accept this point against my argument'?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'a city is not an interface' mean in this essay?
options: Cities do not have digital apps for navigation
A city should not be thought of as a system designed solely for user efficiency; it is an environment shaped by history
Cities are too complex to be described using computer science terminology
Urban planners should not use software when designing new districts
mcQuestions.explanation: An 'interface' is designed for frictionless use — the essay argues that cities are not neutral tools but historical environments full of accidental meaning.
mcQuestions.hint: An interface (like a phone screen) is designed to be as easy as possible — what does it mean to say a city is not that?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best describes a city that is easily and completely understood at a glance?
options: labyrinthine
illegible
legible
serendipitous
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Legible' (in Kevin Lynch's sense) means clearly structured and easy to mentally map — the opposite of 'illegible' or 'labyrinthine'.
mcQuestions.hint: If 'illegible' means hard to read/navigate, which word means the opposite?

options: The philosophical search for meaning in life
The process of navigating or orienting oneself within a built environment
The planning of new road networks in suburban areas
The study of traditional migration routes
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Wayfinding' is the design and experiential practice of how people navigate space — signage, landmarks, spatial cues all contribute to wayfinding.
mcQuestions.hint: Break the word into 'way' (path) + 'finding' — what activity does that describe?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best maintains formal academic register?
options: Old city centres are super cool because you can get lost and find random stuff.
Historic districts are like, totally worth protecting because GPS kills the vibe.
The friction that makes such places difficult is inseparable from the friction that makes them alive.
Getting lost in old cities is fun and that's why we should keep them messy.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses precise vocabulary ('friction', 'inseparable') and a balanced parallel structure appropriate for formal argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid slang, filler intensifiers ('super', 'totally'), and informal contractions.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'frictionless consumption' mean in the context of city tourism?
options: Free public transport that makes cities affordable to visit
A type of retail experience with no checkout queues
The experience of visiting a city designed for easy, effortless tourist movement that removes all obstacles and surprises
Government subsidies that lower the cost of visiting heritage sites
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Frictionless consumption' means experiencing a place without any resistance, effort, or surprise — the essay argues this makes cities interchangeable.
mcQuestions.hint: If 'friction' is resistance or difficulty, what does a city designed to have none of it feel like?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why is the gap 'between past intention and present use' valuable?
options: It provides income for urban archaeologists studying old buildings
It gives city planners useful data about how streets are actually used
It is where spontaneous urban life occurs — the space between what was planned and what actually happens
It identifies areas of a city that require restoration funding
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that cities built by people who 'did not yet know what you would need' from them contain accidental richness that deliberate design cannot reproduce.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the sentence: 'That gap between past intention and present use is precisely where urban life happens.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a place you know that resists easy understanding — a neighbourhood, a building, a landscape. What did it take to begin to understand it, and what did that process give you that a map could not?
